title: Briam
subtitle: Greek baked vegetables with olive oil and herbs
excerpt: Briam is a Greek tray of mixed vegetables (courgettes, potatoes, aubergine, peppers, and tomatoes) baked slowly in olive oil until very tender and concentrated. It is a simple dish that relies on the quality of the olive oil and the time you give it in the oven.
sourceNotes: Briam is a Greek variant of the broader Mediterranean family of slow-baked vegetable dishes, related to the French ratatouille and the Turkish türlü. In Greece it is associated with summer, when courgettes, aubergines, and tomatoes are at their best.

recipe.batchNotes: Makes well in a very large tray. Keeps in the fridge for 4 days.

content.text: Briam is a Greek bake of thinly sliced summer vegetables cooked in generous olive oil until very soft, slightly caramelised, and full of flavour. The method is simple: slice, layer, oil, and leave in the oven for an hour and a half. The olive oil is not a garnish here; it is what makes the dish.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Tip all the sliced vegetables and garlic into a large roasting tin. Season generously with salt, pepper, and {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano. Pour {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil over everything and toss well to coat.
Spread the vegetables in a relatively even layer. Don't worry about perfect layering; they will all cook down. Cover the tin tightly with foil.

Bake for 45 minutes covered, then remove the foil and bake for a further 45 minutes until the vegetables are very soft and the ones on top are slightly caramelised. Check the potatoes by pressing with a knife; they should offer no resistance.
Scatter with fresh parsley if using and serve warm or at room temperature. Good with bread and feta alongside.

items.symptom: The vegetables are watery and not caramelised
items.cause: Foil not removed for the second half of baking, or too many vegetables in a shallow layer
items.fix: Remove the foil and return to the oven. Briam needs the final uncovered period to concentrate and caramelise.

items.cause: Sliced too thick, or not enough time
items.fix: Slice potatoes no thicker than 5 mm. If they're still firm after the full time, cover with foil again and return for another 20 minutes.

content.text: Briam is a summer dish in Greek homes and a staple of the Orthodox fasting diet. It is eaten warm, at room temperature, or cold from the fridge the next day. It makes a good main course with bread and cheese, or a side alongside grilled meat or fish. The olive oil it absorbs while cooking is what carries the flavour.
